# Leviticus Chapter 26 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 74r–74v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_026_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_026_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 112–113 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 26 is the great covenant document of the Holiness Code, the theological crown of the entire Mosaic covenant legislation within Leviticus. It functions as the treaty-curses-and-blessings section standard in ancient Near Eastern suzerain-vassal covenants (cf. Hittite treaty formulary): a prologue recalling the relationship (vv.1–2), blessings for obedience (vv.3–13), and graduated curses for disobedience (vv.14–39), followed by provisions for repentance and restoration (vv.40–45). The structure of the curses section is uniquely systematic: YHWH deploys exactly five escalating rounds of judgment, each introduced by the refrain "and if you do not listen to me" and intensified by the phrase "sevenfold for your sins" (sheva al-chattoteikhem, vv.18, 21, 24, 28) — the number seven as the intensifier of the covenantal curse, mirroring the sevenfold structure of the sabbath. The blessings (vv.3–13) cover four domains: agricultural abundance (vv.4–5), peace and security (vv.6–8), population growth and covenant renewal (vv.9–10), and the supreme blessing of the divine Presence dwelling among them (vv.11–13). The curses escalate through: (1) disease and agricultural failure (vv.16–17); (2) drought and defiant hardness (vv.18–20); (3) wild animals devouring children and livestock (vv.21–22); (4) sword, plague, and famine within the besieged city (vv.23–26); (5) utter desolation — the land made desolate, the cities ruined, the people scattered among the nations (vv.27–39). The closing section (vv.40–45) is one of the most remarkable passages in all of Torah: Israel, scattered and broken in exile, confesses their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers (v.40), and YHWH — who has disciplined them — does not reject them utterly (v.44). He remembers his covenant with the ancestors: Jacob first, then Isaac, then Abraham (v.42) — a deliberate reverse-chronological order perhaps reflecting the covenant's rootedness in primordial promise. The final declaration is the Exodus formula: "I am the LORD their God" (v.45). This chapter is thus not primarily a system of rewards and punishments but a theology of YHWH's relentless covenantal fidelity: even the worst curse trajectory ends in divine memory, divine mercy, and divine covenant-keeping. The Hebrew keyword lo-me'astim ve-lo-ge'altim ("I did not reject them and I did not abhor them," v.44) is among the most consoling phrases in all of Scripture. Deuteronomy 28–30 is the Deuteronomic parallel and expansion of this chapter's covenant curse-and-restoration structure.]*

---

## Leviticus 26

**26:1** You shall not make for yourselves idols [lo ta'asu lakhem elilim — לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ לָכֶם אֱלִילִם; elilim — the plural of elil, "idol/nothingness/worthless thing," from '-l-l or a root meaning "null/empty/vain"; the word carries contempt — these are non-entities, nullities masquerading as gods; the opening prohibition of the covenant chapter reaches back to the Decalogue and grounds everything that follows in the foundational anti-idolatry command], and a carved image [ve-pesel — וּפֶסֶל; pesel from p-s-l, "to hew/carve/cut into shape"; a sculpted representation, whether of wood, stone, or metal] or a sacred pillar [u-matsevah — וּמַצֵּבָה; matsevah — "pillar/standing stone/sacred stele," from n-ts-v, "to stand/be stationed upright"; a standing stone erected for cultic veneration; the word elsewhere can refer to the patriarchal memorial-pillars (Gen 28:18; 31:45) which are not condemned — the condemnation here is of Canaanite cultic pillars used in fertility worship] you shall not erect for yourselves [lo taqimu lakhem — לֹא תָקִימוּ לָכֶם; Hifil imperfect of q-w-m, "to raise up/erect/cause to stand"], and a figured stone [ve-even maskith — וְאֶבֶן מַשְׂכִּית; even — "stone"; maskith — from s-k-h, "to look/regard/have in view"; a stone with carved or painted images, perhaps a devotional image-stone or a pavement-mosaic used in worship; the term is rare and may refer to stones bearing figured reliefs or pictographic images for cultic prostration] you shall not place in your land [lo tittenu be-artsekhem — לֹא תִתְּנוּ בְּאַרְצְכֶם; tittenu — Qal imperfect of n-t-n, "to give/place/set"] to bow down upon it [lehishtachavot aleha — לְהִשְׁתַּחֲוֺת עָלֶיהָ; hishtachavot — the Hishtaph'el of ch-w-h, "to bow down/prostrate oneself"; the deepest gesture of submission and worship]; for I am the LORD your God [ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem — כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם; the self-identification formula — the exclusive claim that grounds the prohibition; there is no room for other objects of worship because YHWH already occupies that relational space].

**26:2** My sabbaths you shall keep [et-shabbetotai tishmoru — אֶת-שַׁבְּתֹתַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ; shabbetotai — "my sabbaths," the plural encompasses all sabbath observances: the weekly sabbath, the sabbatical year, and the great festivals; tishmoru — Qal imperfect of sh-m-r, "to guard/keep/observe"; the sabbath and the sanctuary are the two poles of covenant life — time and space — both consecrated to YHWH], and my sanctuary you shall reverence [u-miqdashi tira'u — וּמִקְדָּשִׁי תִּירָאוּ; miqdashi — "my sanctuary/my holy place," from q-d-sh, "to be holy/set apart"; tira'u — Qal imperfect of y-r-', "to fear/revere/stand in awe of"; the sanctuary is not merely a building to be respected — it is to be approached with reverential awe, the embodiment of the divine Presence among them]; I am the LORD [ani YHWH — אֲנִי יְהוָה; the brief self-declaration closes both the recapitulation of the Decalogue's first and fourth commandments; the covenant obligations have been restated; what follows flows from them].

---

**26:3** If in my statutes you walk [im-be-chuqqotai telekhu — אִם-בְּחֻקֹּתַי תֵּלֵכוּ; im — "if" — the conditional that opens the blessing section; the Holiness Code's covenant structure exactly parallels the Hittite suzerain-vassal treaty: stipulations followed by conditional blessings and curses; be-chuqqotai telekhu — "in my statutes you walk" — the walking metaphor (halakh) is characteristic of covenant life; to "walk in" the statutes is not merely external compliance but a habitual, directional movement of life within the framework of YHWH's commands; chuqqotai — "my statutes/my inscribed decrees," from ch-q-q, "to engrave"], and my commandments you keep [ve-et-mitsvotai tishmoru — וְאֶת-מִצְוֺתַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ; mitsvot — "commandments/commands," from ts-w-h, "to command"; tishmoru — "you keep/guard/observe"; the pairing chuqqot-mitsvot represents the whole of covenantal obligation] and you do them [va-asitem otam — וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֹתָם; the third element — doing — insists on practical actualization; hearing and knowing are not enough]:

**26:4** then I will give your rains in their season [ve-natatti gishmeikhem be-ittam — וְנָתַתִּי גִשְׁמֵיכֶם בְּעִתָּם; natatti — Qal perfect of n-t-n, used as a prophetic/conditional perfect, "I will give"; gishmeikhem — "your rains," from geshem, "rain/downpour"; be-ittam — "in their time/in their season," the critical phrase — rain is only a blessing when it falls at the right time; early rain (yoreh) and late rain (malqosh) were the two essential seasons of rainfall in the agricultural calendar of Canaan; covenantal blessing means the land functions as designed], and the land shall give its produce [ve-natnah ha-aretz yevulah — וְנָתְנָה הָאָרֶץ יְבוּלָהּ; yevulah — "her produce/her yield," from y-b-l, "to bring/carry/lead along"; the land's fruit as her contribution; the same root as yovel], and the tree of the field shall give its fruit [ve-ets ha-sadeh yiten piryo — וְעֵץ הַשָּׂדֶה יִתֵּן פִּרְיוֹ; the three-layer agricultural world: rain from above, grain from the field, fruit from the trees — all three aligned in their seasons].

**26:5** And your threshing shall overtake the vintage [ve-hissig lakhem dayish et-batsir — וְהִשִּׂיג לָכֶם דַּיִשׁ אֶת-בָּצִיר; hissig — Hifil perfect of n-s-g, "to reach/overtake/catch up to"; dayish — "threshing/threshing time," the grain harvest; batsir — "vintage/grape harvest"; the grain threshing lasts so long that it runs into the grape harvest — an image of overabundance: there is so much grain to thresh that you are still at it when the grapes are ripe], and the vintage shall overtake the sowing [ve-batsir yassig et-zara' — וְבָצִיר יַשִּׂיג אֶת-זָרַע; the grape harvest likewise extends until sowing time — the agricultural year is one continuous cycle of abundance with no gap, no hunger season; the image is of a land so productive that each season's activity has not ended before the next begins], and you shall eat your bread to satisfaction [va-akhaltem lachmekhem la-sova' — וַאֲכַלְתֶּם לַחְמְכֶם לָשֹׂבַע; la-sova' — "to satiety/fullness/satisfaction," from s-b-', "to be sated/satisfied"; bread — the basic staple — in abundance], and you shall dwell in your land in safety [vi-shavtem le-vetach be-artsekhem — וִישַׁבְתֶּם לָבֶטַח בְּאַרְצְכֶם; la-vetach — "in security/in confidence," from b-t-ch, "to trust"; the covenantal state: full bellies and no fear].

**26:6** And I will give peace in the land [ve-natatti shalom ba-aretz — וְנָתַתִּי שָׁלוֹם בָּאָרֶץ; shalom — "peace/wholeness/well-being/completeness," not merely the absence of war but the presence of integral flourishing; YHWH gives peace as he gives rain — it is his gift, not Israel's achievement], and you shall lie down with none making you afraid [u-shkhavtem ve-ein macharid — וּשְׁכַבְתֶּם וְאֵין מַחֲרִיד; shkhavtem — Qal perfect of sh-k-v, "to lie down/recline"; macharid — Hifil participle of ch-r-d, "to make tremble/to make afraid/to disturb"; the image of lying down in peace — unguarded sleep — is the quintessential image of security throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ps 4:9; Mic 4:4)]; and I will remove evil beasts from the land [ve-hishbatti chayah ra'ah min-ha-aretz — וְהִשְׁבַּתִּי חַיָּה רָעָה מִן-הָאָרֶץ; hishbatti — Hifil perfect of sh-b-t, "to cause to cease/to remove/to put an end to"; chayah ra'ah — "evil beast/harmful wild creature"; the dangerous animals — lions, bears, serpents — are removed from the land as a dimension of the covenant peace; notably, in the curse section (v.22) these same animals are unleashed as instruments of judgment], and a sword shall not pass through your land [ve-cherev lo-ta'avor be-artsekhem — וְחֶרֶב לֹא-תַעֲבֹר בְּאַרְצְכֶם; cherev — "sword," the instrument of military violence; the sword passing through a land is idiomatic for invasion and conquest; under covenantal blessing, the land is inviolate].

**26:7** And you shall pursue your enemies [ve-raddaftem et-oyveikhem — וּרְדַפְתֶּם אֶת-אֹיְבֵיכֶם; raddaftem — Qal perfect of r-d-f, "to pursue/chase/follow after"; the verb of military pursuit — Israel as the aggressor against those who threaten them], and they shall fall before you by the sword [ve-naflu lifneikhem le-charev — וְנָפְלוּ לִפְנֵיכֶם לֶחָרֶב; naflu — Qal perfect of n-f-l, "to fall"; le-charev — "by the sword/to the sword"; the military victory formula].

**26:8** And five of you shall pursue a hundred [ve-radfu mikem chamishshah me'ah — וְרָדְפוּ מִכֶּם חֲמִשָּׁה מֵאָה; the first military ratio: five Israelites to one hundred enemies — a ratio of 20:1; cf. the escalating ratios elsewhere], and a hundred of you shall pursue ten thousand [u-me'ah mikem revavah yirddofu — וּמֵאָה מִכֶּם רְבָבָה יִרְדֹּפוּ; revavah — "ten thousand/a myriad," from r-b-b, "to be many/numerous"; the second ratio: one hundred to ten thousand — a ratio of 100:1; the escalation from 20:1 to 100:1 is not arithmetic but exponential, suggesting that covenantal blessing multiplies military effectiveness supernaturally — the warrior-ratio grows faster than the numbers], and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword [ve-naflu oyveikhem lifneikhem le-charev — וְנָפְלוּ אֹיְבֵיכֶם לִפְנֵיכֶם לֶחָרֶב].

**26:9** And I will turn toward you [ve-paniti aleikhem — וּפָנִיתִי אֲלֵיכֶם; paniti — Qal perfect of p-n-h, "to turn/face/direct one's face toward"; YHWH turning his face toward Israel is the supreme expression of favor — the opposite of "hiding the face" (haster panim) which is the expression of abandonment; cf. the Aaronic blessing: ya'er YHWH panav elekha, "may the LORD make his face shine upon you" (Num 6:25)] and make you fruitful [ve-hifreiti etkhem — וְהִפְרֵיתִי אֶתְכֶם; hifreiti — Hifil perfect of p-r-h, "to bear fruit/be fruitful"; the Hifil: "I will cause you to bear fruit/make you fruitful"; the creation-blessing of Gen 1:28 (peru u-revu, "be fruitful and multiply") is re-enacted as a covenant blessing], and I will multiply you [ve-hirbeti etkhem — וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶתְכֶם; hirbeti — Hifil perfect of r-b-h, "to become many/increase"; the creation mandate restored as covenant promise], and I will uphold my covenant with you [va-haqimoti et-beriti ittekhem — וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם; haqimoti — Hifil perfect of q-w-m, "to cause to stand/establish/uphold/fulfill"; the covenant is not merely renewed but made to stand firm; beriti — "my covenant" — the specific relationship between YHWH and Israel established at Sinai].

**26:10** And you shall eat the old [stored produce] [va-akhaltem yashan noshain — וַאֲכַלְתֶּם יָשָׁן נוֹשָׁן; yashan noshain — "old old-store/stored old grain"; yashan — "old/year-old," Qal adjective of y-sh-n, "to be old/sleep"; noshain — Hifil participle or variant form, emphasizing the fully-aged stored reserves; the intensive doubling: the harvest is so abundant that you are still eating last year's previous stored grain when the new harvest comes in — there is simply nowhere to put the fresh grain], and the old you shall bring out from before the new [ve-yashan mippenei chadash totsi'u — וְיָשָׁן מִפְּנֵי חָדָשׁ תּוֹצִיאוּ; mippenei chadash — "from before the new/to make room for the new"; you must carry out the old stored grain to make space for the incoming new harvest — the barns overflow; the image is of super-abundance: old grain displaced by incoming new harvest].

**26:11** And I will set my dwelling in your midst [ve-natatti mishkani be-tokhekhem — וְנָתַתִּי מִשְׁכָּנִי בְּתוֹכְכֶם; mishkani — "my dwelling/my tabernacle," from sh-k-n, "to dwell/settle/tabernacle"; the root of mishkan, "Tabernacle"; be-tokhekhem — "in your midst/in the center of you"; this is the supreme covenant blessing — YHWH's own Presence dwelling spatially among his people; all the agricultural and military blessings pale beside this; the Tabernacle already stands among them, but this promises that this arrangement will continue as a permanent covenantal reality], and my soul shall not reject you [ve-lo-tig'al nafshi etkhem — וְלֹא-תִגְעַל נַפְשִׁי אֶתְכֶם; tig'al — Qal imperfect of g-'-l, "to abhor/loathe/be disgusted with/reject with revulsion"; nafshi — "my soul/my being/my inner self"; YHWH speaks with full emotional interiority — his nafshi, his deepest being, will not abhor them; the verb g-'-l connotes visceral disgust, the strongest possible expression of rejection; the negated promise is profound: however much Israel fails, YHWH's inmost being will not reach that point of revulsion].

**26:12** And I will walk among you [ve-hithalakhti be-tokhekhem — וְהִתְהַלַּכְתִּי בְּתוֹכְכֶם; hithalakhti — Hitpa'el perfect of h-l-kh, "to walk about/walk to and fro/walk habitually"; the Hitpa'el (reflexive-intensive) of halakh suggests continuous, habitual walking — YHWH walking back and forth through the camp as a present, active participant in the community's life; the same Hitpa'el appears in Gen 3:8 — the LORD walking about (hithalekh) in the garden in the evening breeze; covenantal blessing recapitulates Eden; be-tokhekhem — "in the midst of you/among you"], and I will be your God [ve-hayiti lakhem le-Elohim — וְהָיִיתִי לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים; the covenant formula], and you — you shall be my people [ve-atem tihyu-li le-am — וְאַתֶּם תִּהְיוּ-לִי לְעָם; the double formula — the Bundesformel, the covenant-formula — "I will be your God / you will be my people" — found in various forms throughout the prophets (Jer 7:23; 11:4; Ezek 36:28; Hos 2:23); atem — emphatic "you" — the reciprocity is stressed].

**26:13** I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikhem — אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם] who brought you out [asher hotseti etkhem — אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם; hotseti — Hifil perfect of y-ts-', "to go out"; the Hifil: "who caused you to go out"; the fundamental Exodus-act that grounds all covenant relationship] from the land of Egypt [me-erets Mitsraim — מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם], from being their slaves [mi-heyot lahem avadim — מִהְיוֹת לָהֶם עֲבָדִים; mi-heyot — "from being/from the state of being"; lahem — "to them/as theirs"; avadim — "slaves/servants"; the precise description of their former status — they belonged to the Egyptians as property], and I broke the bars of your yoke [va-eshbor motot olekhem — וָאֶשְׁבֹּר מֹטֹת עֻלְּכֶם; eshbor — Qal imperfect with waw-consecutive of sh-b-r, "to break/shatter"; motot — "bars/carrying-poles," the horizontal bars of a yoke that sit across the shoulders of an ox; olekhem — "your yoke," from '-l-l; the yoke-bar is the symbol of servitude and subjugation (cf. Jer 27:2; Ezek 34:27)] and I made you walk upright [va-olek etkhem qomemiyut — וָאוֹלֵךְ אֶתְכֶם קוֹמְמִיּוּת; olek — Hifil imperfect with waw-consecutive of h-l-kh, "to cause to walk/lead"; qomemiyut — "uprightness/with head erect," from q-w-m, "to stand/rise"; the adverb occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible; it captures the dignified, erect posture of the freed person — no longer bowed under a yoke, no longer bent under servitude, but standing tall; YHWH did not merely liberate Israel — he restored their human dignity, their ability to walk as upright creatures made in the divine image].

---

**26:14** But if you do not listen to me [ve-im-lo tishmeu li — וְאִם-לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ לִי; the great adversative: after all the blessings, the conditional reversal; im-lo — "but if not"; tishmeu — Qal imperfect of sh-m-', "to hear/listen/obey"; li — "to me/unto me"; the covenant demands hearing — shema — and hearing demands obedience; the refusal to hear is the root of all covenant failure], and do not do all these commandments [ve-lo ta'asu et kol-ha-mitsvot ha-eleh — וְלֹא תַעֲשׂוּ אֵת כָּל-הַמִּצְוֺת הָאֵלֶּה; kol — "all" — total obedience is required; partial compliance does not satisfy the covenant; ha-eleh — "these," the demonstrative pointing to the entire body of commandments just given]:

**26:15** and if my statutes you reject [ve-im be-chuqqotai tim'asu — וְאִם-בְּחֻקֹּתַי תִּמְאָסוּ; tim'asu — Qal imperfect of m-'-s, "to reject/despise/refuse/treat as worthless"; the rejection of the statutes is not indifference but active contempt; the same verb appears in v.43 and v.44 — YHWH will not reject Israel even though Israel has rejected him], and if my judgments your soul abhors [ve-im et-mishpatai tig'al nafshekem — וְאִם-אֶת-מִשְׁפָּטַי תִּגְעַל נַפְשְׁכֶם; tig'al — Qal imperfect of g-'-l, "to loathe/abhor"; nafshekem — "your souls/your beings"; the same verb used in v.11 of YHWH's soul not abhorring Israel — the covenant symmetry is stark: if Israel's soul abhors YHWH's judgments, it mirrors exactly what YHWH promises not to do to them], so as not to do all my commandments [le-vilti asot et kol-mitsvotai — לְבִלְתִּי עֲשׂוֹת אֵת-כָּל-מִצְוֺתַי; le-vilti — the negative infinitive particle "so as not to/in order not to"], to break my covenant [le-hafrekhem et-beriti — לְהַפְרְכֶם אֶת-בְּרִיתִי; hafrekhem — Hifil infinitive of p-r-r, "to break/annul/shatter/frustrate/invalidate"; the breaking of the covenant is the culmination of the sequence — rejection of statutes, abhorrence of judgments, non-compliance with commandments all lead to covenant rupture]:

**26:16** then I — I will do this to you [af-ani e'eseh-zot lakhem — אַף-אֲנִי אֶעֱשֶׂה-זֹּאת לָכֶם; af — "also/even," but used here as an intensifier of solemn resolve; ani — emphatic "I" — YHWH himself is the agent of the curses, not merely a passive allowance; the divine first-person active involvement in judgment]: I will appoint over you terror [ve-hifqadeti aleikhem behalah — וְהִפְקַדְתִּי עֲלֵיכֶם בֶּהָלָה; hifqadeti — Hifil perfect of p-q-d, "to appoint/assign/visit/attend to"; behalah — "terror/panic/dread/sudden fright," from b-h-l, "to be dismayed/terrified"; the covenant curse begins with psychological collapse — panic precedes all other disasters], consumption [et-hashachefet — אֶת-הַשַּׁחֶפֶת; hashachefet — "wasting disease/tuberculosis-like consumption/emaciation," from sh-ch-f, "to be worn thin/emaciated"; a wasting illness that wastes the body's substance; the medical curse strikes the body's capacity for health and fertility], and fever [ve-et-haqaddachat — וְאֶת-הַקַּדַּחַת; haqaddachat — "fever/burning disease," from q-d-ch, "to burn/kindle/be inflamed"; the body's heat turned against itself; the pairing consumption + fever covers wasting and burning, the two great forms of disease] that waste the eyes [mekalot einayim — מְכַלּוֹת עֵינַיִם; mekalot — Pi'el participle of k-l-h, "to complete/finish/waste/consume"; the diseases "consume/waste the eyes" — sight fails, perhaps from the prolonged fever and emaciation] and cause grief to the soul [u-medivot nafesh — וּמְדִיבֹת נָפֶשׁ; medivot — Pi'el participle of d-'-v or d-w-v, "to pine/grieve/languish"; nafesh — "soul/inner being/life-force"; the soul itself grieves under these afflictions]. And you shall sow your seed in vain [vi-zara'tem larik zar'akhem — וּזְרַעְתֶּם לָרִיק זַרְעֲכֶם; larik — "in vain/for emptiness/for nothing," from riq, "empty/vain"; the work of planting produces nothing], and your enemies shall eat it [ve-akhaluhu oyveikhem — וַאֲכָלֻהוּ אֹיְבֵיכֶם; the fruits of labor harvested by those who hate you — the inversion of v.5's promise of eating your own bread to satisfaction].

**26:17** And I will set my face against you [ve-natatti panai bakhem — וְנָתַתִּי פָנַי בָּכֶם; natatti panai — literally "I will give my face against you"; to "set/give the face against" someone is to direct concentrated divine attention toward them in hostile judgment; the face of YHWH in blessing "turns toward" (panah el, v.9); in judgment it is "set against" (natan panim be-)], and you shall be struck down before your enemies [ve-nigaftem lifnei oyveikhem — וְנִגַּפְתֶּם לִפְנֵי אֹיְבֵיכֶם; nigaftem — Nifal perfect of n-g-f, "to be struck/be defeated/be plagued"; the same root as maggefah, "plague/blow"; you shall be struck down in military defeat — the exact inversion of v.7's promise that enemies fall before you], and your enemies shall rule over you [ve-radu vakhem sone'ekhem — וְרָדוּ בָכֶם שֹׂנְאֵיכֶם; radu — Qal perfect of r-d-h, "to rule/have dominion/tread down"; sone'ekhem — "those who hate you"; the reversal: those who hate you will rule over you instead of falling before you], and you shall flee when none pursues you [ve-nastem ve-ein-rodef etkhem — וְנַסְתֶּם וְאֵין-רֹדֵף אֶתְכֶם; nastem — Qal perfect of n-w-s, "to flee/take flight"; rodef — Qal participle of r-d-f, "to pursue"; the profound psychological curse — the enemy need not even chase them; covenantal guilt creates internal panic that drives them to flee from nothing; cf. Prov 28:1 "the wicked flee when none pursues"].

---

**26:18** And if after these things [ve-im-ad-elleh — וְאִם-עַד-אֵלֶּה; the second curse stage begins; ad-elleh — "even to these things/even up to this point"; despite the first stage of curses] you do not listen to me [lo tishmeu li — לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ לִי], then I will add [ve-yasafti — וְיָסַפְתִּי; Hifil perfect of y-s-f, "to add/increase/do again"] to discipline you sevenfold for your sins [le-yassarah etkhem sheva al-chattoteikhem — לְיַסְּרָה אֶתְכֶם שֶׁבַע עַל-חַטֹּאתֵיכֶם; yassarah — Pi'el infinitive of y-s-r, "to discipline/chasten/instruct through affliction"; sheva — "seven/sevenfold"; al-chattoteikhem — "for/upon your sins"; the sevenfold discipline: the number seven as the covenantal multiplier, the currency of covenant enforcement; what was already severe is now seven times more severe].

**26:19** And I will break the pride of your strength [ve-shavarti et-ge'on uzekhem — וְשָׁבַרְתִּי אֶת-גְּאוֹן עֻזְּכֶם; shavarti — Qal perfect of sh-b-r, "to break/shatter"; ge'on — "pride/majesty/exaltation," from g-'-h, "to be high/exalted"; uzekhem — "your strength/your power"; ge'on uz — "the pride of strength" — the confident, self-reliant pride in one's own power; in the blessings section the land gave its strength; now YHWH breaks that strength at its root], and I will make your heavens like iron [ve-natatti et-shemeikhem ka-barzel — וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-שְׁמֵיכֶם כַּבַּרְזֶל; shemeikhem — "your sky/your heavens"; ka-barzel — "like iron/as iron"; an iron sky = a sky that withholds all rain — impenetrable, unyielding, merciless] and your land like bronze [ve-artsekhem ka-nechushshah — וְאַרְצְכֶם כַּנְּחוּשָׁה; ka-nechushshah — "like bronze/as bronze/as copper"; the paired metaphors: iron sky (no rain from above) and bronze ground (hard, unyielding, refusing to receive or give moisture); the entire agricultural system is shut down — the land's covenant productivity reversed completely; cf. Deut 28:23 for the identical image].

**26:20** And your strength shall be spent in vain [ve-tamm larik kochakem — וְתַם לָרִיק כֹּחֲכֶם; tamm — Qal perfect of t-m-m, "to be complete/come to an end/be spent/exhausted"; larik — "in vain/for emptiness"; kochakem — "your strength/your power"; the bodily strength that goes into agricultural labor is exhausted for nothing], for your land shall not give its produce [ve-lo titen artsekhem et-yevulah — וְלֹא תִתֵּן אַרְצְכֶם אֶת-יְבוּלָהּ; yevulah — "her produce/her yield," as in v.4; the land promised to yield in the blessings (v.4) is now explicitly described as failing to yield], and the tree of the land shall not give its fruit [ve-ets ha-aretz lo yiten piryo — וְעֵץ הָאָרֶץ לֹא יִתֵּן פִּרְיוֹ; the same triad from v.4 — rains, land's produce, trees' fruit — now inverted in sequence as curses; all three levels of agricultural provision withdrawn].

---

**26:21** And if you walk contrary to me [ve-im-telekhu imi qeri — וְאִם-תֵּלְכוּ עִמִּי קֶרִי; qeri — one of the most debated words in this chapter: from q-r-h, "to encounter/happen/meet by chance," the word seems to denote a casual, accidental, or hostile manner of relating; several interpretations exist: (a) "by chance/randomly" — treating the covenant relationship as if its outcomes are random and YHWH's actions arbitrary; (b) "in hostility/in opposition" — from a root meaning "to be hard/hostile"; (c) "stubbornly/with a hardened neck"; the most likely sense is "casually/indifferently/treating my acts as if they were random natural events rather than divine responses"; the Rabbis read qeri as related to mikreh, "chance/happenstance" — Israel treats YHWH's discipline as random misfortune rather than covenantal discipline, and therefore does not repent; the word appears seven times in this chapter (vv.21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41) — it is the chapter's own keyword for the attitude that escalates the curse cycle] and are not willing to listen to me [ve-lo tovu lishmoa li — וְלֹא תֹאבוּ לִשְׁמֹעַ לִי; tovu — Qal imperfect of '-b-h, "to be willing/to consent/to yield"; li-shmoa — "to listen"; the refusal is now volitional and deliberate — not inability but unwillingness], then I will add upon you strokes sevenfold according to your sins [ve-yasafti aleikhem makkah sheva ke-chattoteikhem — וְיָסַפְתִּי עֲלֵיכֶם מַכָּה שֶׁבַע כְּחַטֹּאתֵיכֶם; makkah — "blow/stroke/plague/wound/smiting," from n-k-h, "to strike"; sheva — sevenfold; ke-chattoteikhem — "according to your sins," shifting from al (v.18, "upon your sins") to ke- ("according to/in proportion to your sins"); the discipline is now calibrated precisely to the degree of their sin].

**26:22** And I will send against you the beast of the field [ve-shalachti vakhem et-chayat ha-sadeh — וְשָׁלַחְתִּי בָכֶם אֶת-חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה; shalachti — Qal perfect of sh-l-ch, "to send/release/let loose"; the wild animals removed from the land as a blessing (v.6) are now sent against them as a curse; the blessing and curse are perfectly symmetrical], and it shall bereave you of your children [ve-shikkelah etkhem — וְשִׁכְּלָה אֶתְכֶם; shikkelah — Pi'el perfect of sh-k-l, "to make childless/bereave of children"; the devouring of children by wild animals is the most devastating form of loss — the future of the people destroyed], and it shall cut off your livestock [ve-hikhrita et-behemtekhem — וְהִכְרִיתָה אֶת-בְּהֶמְתְּכֶם; hikhrita — Hifil perfect of k-r-t, "to cut off/destroy/eliminate"; behemtekhem — "your livestock"; both sources of the future are attacked: children (human continuity) and livestock (economic continuity)], and it shall make you few [ve-him'ita etkhem — וְהִמְעִיטָה אֶתְכֶם; him'ita — Hifil perfect of m-'-t, "to make few/reduce/diminish"; the reversal of the blessing "I will multiply you" (v.9)], and your roads shall become desolate [ve-nashammu darkeikhem — וְנָשַׁמּוּ דַּרְכֵיכֶם; nashammu — Nifal perfect of sh-m-m, "to be desolate/be appalled/be waste"; darkeikhem — "your roads/your paths"; roads emptied of travelers because the land is dangerous and people have been killed or fled; the desolate road is the image of social and economic collapse].

---

**26:23** And if by these things you are not disciplined toward me [ve-im-be-elleh lo tivasru li — וְאִם-בְּאֵלֶּה לֹא תִוָּסְרוּ לִי; tivasru — Nifal imperfect of y-s-r, "to be disciplined/chastened/corrected/instructed"; the passive: "you are not disciplined/corrected by these"; li — "toward me/unto me"; the purpose of the disciplining is repentance and return to YHWH, not merely behavioral modification; if the discipline does not produce that return, it must escalate] and you walk contrary to me [ve-halakhtem imi qeri — וַהֲלַכְתֶּם עִמִּי קֶרִי; the third occurrence of qeri; their fundamental attitude — treating YHWH's acts as random — remains unchanged]:

**26:24** then I — I also will walk contrary to you [ve-halakhti af-ani imakem be-qeri — וְהָלַכְתִּי אַף-אֲנִי עִמָּכֶם בְּקֶרִי; af-ani — emphatic "I also/even I"; YHWH mirrors their behavior — if they treat him with qeri, he will treat them with qeri; the lex talionis of covenant relationship; the divine response is not arbitrary but proportional and symmetrical], and I — I will strike you sevenfold for your sins [ve-hikketi etkhem gam-ani sheva al-chattoteikhem — וְהִכֵּיתִי אֶתְכֶם גַּם-אֲנִי שֶׁבַע עַל-חַטֹּאתֵיכֶם; hikketi — Hifil perfect of n-k-h, "to strike/smite/wound/plague"; gam-ani — "I also/I too"; the personal divine involvement in smiting is stressed three times in this verse — "I," "even I," "I also"].

**26:25** And I will bring a sword upon you [ve-heveti aleikhem cherev — וְהֵבֵאתִי עֲלֵיכֶם חֶרֶב; the sword — military invasion and slaughter — is the instrument of the fourth curse stage], avenging the vengeance of the covenant [noqemet neqam-berit — נֹקֶמֶת נְקַם-בְּרִית; noqemet — Qal participle of n-q-m, "to avenge/take vengeance/requite"; neqam-berit — "the vengeance of the covenant/covenant-vengeance/the punishment that belongs to the covenant"; the sword is not general military defeat but specifically the covenant's own enforcement mechanism; the covenant itself demands and produces this reckoning; the concept of covenant-vengeance is attested in ancient Near Eastern treaties where the gods of the treaty are called to witness and to avenge violations]; and you shall be gathered into your cities [ve-ne'esaftem el-areikhem — וְנֶאֱסַפְתֶּם אֶל-עָרֵיכֶם; ne'esaftem — Nifal perfect of '-s-f, "to be gathered/be collected"; the gathering into cities anticipates siege — the population flees to walled cities for protection from the invading army], and I will send pestilence in your midst [ve-shilachti dever be-tokhekhem — וְשִׁלַּחְתִּי דֶבֶר בְּתוֹכְכֶם; dever — "pestilence/plague/epidemic disease," one of the four great biblical judgment instruments: sword, famine, pestilence, wild beasts (cf. Ezek 14:21); in the city crowded with refugees, disease spreads rapidly], and you shall be given into the hand of the enemy [ve-nittanem be-yad-oyev — וְנִתַּתֶּם בְּיַד-אוֹיֵב; nittanem — Nifal perfect of n-t-n, "to be given/handed over/delivered"; the passive form — YHWH gives them over; the ultimate military surrender].

**26:26** When I break your staff of bread [be-shivri lakhem matteh-lechem — בְּשִׁבְרִי לָכֶם מַטֵּה-לָחֶם; shivri — Qal infinitive construct of sh-b-r, "to break/shatter" with first-person suffix: "in my breaking"; matteh-lechem — "staff of bread/bread-staff"; matteh — "staff/rod/support," from n-t-h, "to stretch/extend"; the staff of bread is bread understood as the staff one leans on — the support of life; the idiom captures the essential role of bread as the basic means of sustaining life; when YHWH "breaks the staff of bread," the food supply collapses], and ten women shall bake your bread in one oven [ve-affu eser nashim lachmekhem be-tannur echad — וְאָפוּ עֶשֶׂר נָשִׁים לַחְמְכֶם בְּתַנּוּר אֶחָד; eser nashim — "ten women"; tannur echad — "one oven"; normally each household had its own oven; ten families sharing one oven means ten families' worth of bread-baking has been reduced to one oven's capacity — a fraction of the normal production], and they shall give back your bread by weight [ve-heishivu lachmekhem ba-mishqal — וְהֵשִׁיבוּ לַחְמְכֶם בַּמִּשְׁקָל; ba-mishqal — "by weight/by measure"; in times of severe scarcity, bread is doled out by careful weighing — each person receives their exactly measured ration; the image of bread-by-weight is an image of famine-rationing], and you shall eat and not be satisfied [va-akhaltem ve-lo tisba'u — וַאֲכַלְתֶּם וְלֹא תִשְׂבָּעוּ; the inversion of the covenant blessing "you shall eat and be satisfied to fullness" (v.5); eating without satisfaction — the food is insufficient to fill the body's need; a curse of emptiness alongside physical eating].

---

**26:27** And if in this you do not listen to me [ve-im-be-zot lo tishmeu li — וְאִם-בְּזֹאת לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ לִי; the fifth and final curse stage; be-zot — "in this/by all this"; the culminating "if"], and you walk contrary to me [ve-halakhtem imi be-qeri — וַהֲלַכְתֶּם עִמִּי בְּקֶרִי]:

**26:28** then I will walk contrary to you in fury [ve-halakhti imakem ba-chamat-qeri — וְהָלַכְתִּי עִמָּכֶם בַּחֲמַת-קֶרִי; ba-chamat-qeri — "in the heat-of-wrath of contrariness/in furious contrariness"; chamat — "heat/wrath/fury," the burning intensity of the divine anger; qeri — the hostile-indifference now escalated to the divine fury; this is the strongest expression of divine anger in the curse series], and I also will discipline you sevenfold for your sins [ve-yissarti etkhem af-ani sheva al-chattoteikhem — וְיִסַּרְתִּי אֶתְכֶם אַף-אֲנִי שֶׁבַע עַל-חַטֹּאתֵיכֶם; the final sevenfold — the full force of covenantal discipline].

**26:29** And you shall eat the flesh of your sons [va-akhaltem besar beneikhem — וַאֲכַלְתֶּם בְּשַׂר בְּנֵיכֶם; the most horrifying of the covenant curses — cannibalism driven by starvation during siege; the flesh of children, the dearest possession, consumed for survival; this curse is not hyperbolic: it is attested historically — 2 Kgs 6:28–29 records precisely this occurring during the siege of Samaria; Lam 4:10 records it during the siege of Jerusalem], and the flesh of your daughters you shall eat [u-vesar benoteikhem tokhelu — וּבְשַׂר בְּנֹתֵיכֶם תֹּאכֵלוּ; the gendered doubling — sons and daughters — ensures no one misses the completeness of the horror; the covenant curse has reached its uttermost point].

**26:30** And I will destroy your high places [ve-hishmadeti et-bamoteikhem — וְהִשְׁמַדְתִּי אֶת-בָּמֹתֵיכֶם; bamot — "high places/cultic platforms/worship sites," from b-m-h, "to be high"; the plural of bamah, "high place" — the open-air worship sites scattered across Canaan used for both YHWH-worship and Canaanite deity worship; the destruction of the high places is an act of judgment on syncretic worship], and I will cut down your incense altars [ve-hikhrati et-hammanekhem — וְהִכְרַתִּי אֶת-חַמָּנֵיכֶם; hammanim — "incense altars/sun-pillars/incense stands," from chamah, "sun/heat"; the precise referent is debated — they may be incense stands dedicated to solar deities, or simply incense altars; the term appears frequently in prophetic condemnations of Canaanite worship (Ezek 6:4; Isa 17:8)], and I will cast your corpses upon the corpses of your idols [ve-natatti et-pigreichem al-pigrei giluleikhem — וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-פִּגְרֵיכֶם עַל-פִּגְרֵי גִּלּוּלֵיכֶם; pigrim — "corpses/carcasses/dead bodies"; gilulim — "idols/dungy things/dung-pellets," one of the most contemptuous terms for idols in the Hebrew Bible, from g-l-l, "to roll/dung" — the same root as dung (galal); the word may evoke stone rollers or dung-shaped objects; it appears over forty times in Ezekiel as his characteristic term of scorn for idols; their corpses thrown upon the idols their makers — the ultimate irony and desecration], and my soul shall abhor you [ve-ga'alah nafshi etkhem — וְגָעֲלָה נַפְשִׁי אֶתְכֶם; ga'alah — Qal perfect of g-'-l, "to abhor/loathe/be disgusted with"; nafshi — "my soul/my innermost being"; the conditional extreme: YHWH's nafshi reaches the point of revulsion that v.11 promised would not happen — the conditional horror of what covenant breach leads to at its worst; yet even this is not the last word, as vv.40–45 will demonstrate].

**26:31** And I will make your cities a waste [ve-natatti et-areikhem chorvah — וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-עָרֵיכֶם חָרְבָּה; chorvah — "ruin/desolation/waste/desert," from ch-r-v, "to be dry/desolate/ruined"; the physical destruction of urban infrastructure], and I will make your sanctuaries desolate [va-hashimoti et-miqdashekhem — וַהֲשִׁמֹּתִי אֶת-מִקְדְּשֵׁיכֶם; hashimoti — Hifil perfect of sh-m-m, "to make desolate/cause to be appalled/lay waste"; miqdashekhem — "your sanctuaries/your holy places," plural; the places where they met with YHWH rendered desolate], and I will not smell your soothing aromas [ve-lo arach be-reyach nichokhekhem — וְלֹא אָרִיחַ בְּרֵיחַ נִיחֹחֲכֶם; arach — Hifil imperfect of r-y-ch, "to smell/inhale/take pleasure in the scent"; reyach nichoach — "soothing aroma/pleasing odor/restful smell," the standard technical phrase for sacrifice (Gen 8:21; throughout Leviticus); YHWH will no longer smell, no longer take pleasure in, no longer be appeased by their sacrifices; the rejection of the cult is total — the entire sacrificial system becomes meaningless when the relationship it presupposes has been destroyed].

**26:32** And I will make the land desolate [va-hashimoti ani et-ha-aretz — וַהֲשִׁמֹּתִי אֲנִי אֶת-הָאָרֶץ; ani — emphatic "I myself"; YHWH personally makes the land desolate], and your enemies who dwell in it shall be appalled by it [ve-shamevu alehah oyveikhem ha-yoshvim bah — וְשָׁמְמוּ עָלֶיהָ אֹיְבֵיכֶם הַיֹּשְׁבִים בָּהּ; shamevu — Qal perfect of sh-m-m, "to be appalled/be desolate/be devastated"; the conquerors who settle in the desolated land will themselves be appalled by what they find — the land is so desolate that even its new inhabitants are horrified; the land has been punished, not merely handed over to new occupants].

**26:33** And you — I will scatter among the nations [ve-etkhem azareh ba-goyyim — וְאֶתְכֶם אֱזָרֶה בַגּוֹיִם; azareh — Qal imperfect of z-r-h, "to scatter/winnow/disperse," a term from agriculture — winnowing grain by tossing it in the wind so the chaff blows away; the people scattered like chaff; ba-goyyim — "among the nations"], and I will draw out a sword after you [ve-hareqoti achareichem cherev — וַהֲרִיקֹתִי אַחֲרֵיכֶם חָרֶב; hareqoti — Hifil perfect of r-w-q, "to empty/draw out/unsheathe"; achareichem — "after you/behind you/pursuing you"; the drawn sword pursues the scattered people through their exile], and your land shall be a desolation [ve-hayetah artsekhem shemamah — וְהָיְתָה אַרְצְכֶם שְׁמָמָה; shemamah — "desolation/waste/emptiness"], and your cities shall be a waste [ve-areikhem yihyu chorvah — וְעָרֵיכֶם יִהְיוּ חָרְבָּה; the completeness of the devastation: people scattered, land desolate, cities ruined].

**26:34** Then the land shall be paid its sabbaths [az tirtsah ha-aretz et-shabbetotehah — אָז תִּרְצֶה הָאָרֶץ אֶת-שַׁבְּתֹתֶיהָ; tirtsah — Qal imperfect of r-ts-h, "to be pleased/be satisfied/be paid/be compensated for"; the land "receives its compensation" — what was owed to it and withheld during Israel's settlement is now repaid through the emptiness of exile; shabbetotehah — "her sabbaths," the sabbatical years that the people refused to observe; throughout the Leviticus 25 sabbatical legislation the land had its own rights and needs; now in exile the land receives what was denied it] all the days of its desolation [kol-yemei hashammah — כָּל-יְמֵי הַשַּׁמָּה; hashammah — "the desolation/the desolate period"; every year of exile counts as a sabbath-rest for the land], while you are in the land of your enemies [ve-atem bi-erets oyveikhem — וְאַתֶּם בְּאֶרֶץ אֹיְבֵיכֶם; the harsh juxtaposition: while the land rests in peace (its sabbath), Israel suffers in alien territory]; then the land shall rest [az tishbot ha-aretz — אָז תִּשְׁבֹּת הָאָרֶץ; tishbot — Qal imperfect of sh-b-t, "to rest/cease/keep sabbath"], and it shall be paid its sabbaths [ve-hirtsah et-shabbetotehah — וְהִרְצָת אֶת-שַׁבְּתֹתֶיהָ; the payment/satisfaction formula repeated; 2 Chr 36:21 cites this verse explicitly to explain the seventy-year Babylonian exile: "to fulfill the word of the LORD by the mouth of Jeremiah, until the land had made up for its sabbaths"].

**26:35** All the days that it lies desolate [kol-yemei hashammah tishbot — כָּל-יְמֵי הַשַּׁמָּה תִּשְׁבֹּת; the duration of the land's sabbath rest equals the duration of the exile — a precise moral accounting], it shall rest what it did not rest in your sabbaths [et asher-lo shavta be-shabbetoteikhem — אֵת אֲשֶׁר-לֹא שָׁבְתָה בְּשַׁבְּתֹתֵיכֶם; shavta — Qal perfect of sh-b-t; the unpaid sabbath-debts from the settlement period are now exactly repaid through exile; the mathematics of covenant consequence is precise and unerring; be-shabbetoteikhem — "in your sabbaths/during your sabbath-times"].

---

**26:36** And as for those who remain among you [ve-ha-nish'arim bakem — וְהַנִּשְׁאָרִים בָּכֶם; nish'arim — Nifal participle of sh-'-r, "to remain/be left/survive"; those who survive the sword, plague, famine, and deportation — the remnant left in the land or scattered in exile] — I will bring faintness into their heart in the lands of their enemies [ve-heveti morekh bi-levavam be-artsot oyveikhem — וְהֵבֵאתִי מֹרֶךְ בִּלְבָבָם בְּאַרְצֹת אֹיְבֵיכֶם; morekh — "faintness/softness/timidity/cowardice," from r-k-k, "to be soft/tender/faint-hearted"; bi-levavam — "in their heart/in their inner being"; the covenantal curse of the heart — the inner person is made craven; cf. Deut 20:8, where the "faint-hearted" (rakh ha-levav) man is sent home from battle], and the sound of a driven leaf shall pursue them [ve-radaf otam qol aleh niddaf — וְרָדַף אֹתָם קוֹל עָלֶה נִדָּף; radaf — Qal perfect of r-d-f, "to pursue"; qol — "sound/voice"; aleh niddaf — "a blown/driven leaf/a rustling leaf," from n-d-f, "to be blown/scattered/driven by wind"; the exquisite cruelty of this image: the scattered, faint-hearted exiles flee in terror from the sound of a wind-blown leaf; no actual enemy pursues them — only the noise of a leaf driven by the wind; the internal panic is so complete that the slightest sound triggers flight; cf. v.17 where they flee when none pursues — here the cause of the flight is identified as a leaf-sound; this is the full flowering of the curse of v.17], and they shall flee as one flees from the sword [ve-nasu manus-cherev — וְנָסוּ מְנוּס-חָרֶב; manus-cherev — "the flight of the sword/fleeing as from a sword/as one flees from the sword"; the cognate construction], and they shall fall when none pursues [ve-nafelu ve-ein rodef — וְנָפְלוּ וְאֵין רֹדֵף; the realization of v.17's prediction in its most extreme form — falling in fear from nothing].

**26:37** And they shall stumble [ve-kashelu — וְכָשְׁלוּ; Qal perfect of k-sh-l, "to stumble/totter/fall"], a man over his brother [ish be-achiv — אִישׁ בְּאָחִיו; "man against his brother/one against another"; the community that was supposed to be a brotherhood has collapsed into a crowd of panicked individuals tripping over each other in flight], as from before a sword [ki-lifnei-cherev — כִּלִּפְנֵי-חָרֶב; as if running from the sword], though none pursues [ve-ein rodef — וְאֵין רֹדֵף; the refrain repeated; what drives them is internal terror, not external threat]. And you shall have no standing before your enemies [ve-lo tihyeh lakhem teqhumah lifnei oyveikhem — וְלֹא תִהְיֶה לָכֶם תְּקוּמָה לִפְנֵי אֹיְבֵיכֶם; teqhumah — "standing/the ability to stand firm/power to stand up," from q-w-m, "to stand/rise"; the word occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible; the complete loss of the ability to stand before enemies — military incapacity stemming from inner moral and spiritual collapse].

**26:38** And you shall perish among the nations [va-avadtem ba-goyyim — וַאֲבַדְתֶּם בַּגּוֹיִם; avadtem — Qal perfect of '-b-d, "to perish/be lost/be destroyed"; among the nations — the scattered exile becomes dissolution; the ultimate threat to covenantal identity is not merely defeat but disappearance], and the land of your enemies shall devour you [ve-akhlah etkhem erets oyveikhem — וְאָכְלָה אֶתְכֶם אֶרֶץ אֹיְבֵיכֶם; the land itself described as devouring — the same consuming activity that their own covenant land should have done in fertility but now their enemies' land does in destruction; cf. Lev 18:28 where the land "vomits out" its inhabitants for sexual impurity — land as active moral agent].

**26:39** And those of you who remain [ve-ha-nish'arim bakhem — וְהַנִּשְׁאָרִים בָּכֶם; the survivors of the exile process — those not yet fully consumed] shall rot away [yimmaqequ — יִמָּקֲקוּ; Niphal imperfect of m-q-q, "to rot/decay/waste away/pine/melt"; the verb implies a slow, gradual dissolution — rotting like organic matter, wasting away in helplessness] in their iniquity [ba-avonam — בַּעֲוֺנָם; ba-avonam — "in their iniquity/under the weight of their guilt"; the iniquity is what they are rotting in — their sin as an environment that corrupts them from within], in the lands of your enemies [be-artsot oyveikhem — בְּאַרְצֹת אֹיְבֵיכֶם]; and also in the iniquities of their fathers with them they shall rot [ve-af ba-avonot avotam ittam yimmaqequ — וְאַף בַּעֲוֺנֹת אֲבֹתָם אִתָּם יִמָּקֵּקוּ; af — "also/moreover"; avotam — "their fathers"; ittam — "with them"; the accumulated iniquity of generations compounds — they bear not only their own iniquity but their fathers', the weight of generational covenant failure; cf. Exod 20:5, Deut 5:9 — the iniquities of the fathers visited upon children; this is not arbitrary blame-transfer but the recognition that covenantal patterns of unfaithfulness are transmitted and intensified across generations].

---

**26:40** But if they confess their iniquity [ve-hitvaddu et-avonam — וְהִתְוַדּוּ אֶת-עֲוֺנָם; hitvaddu — Hitpa'el perfect of y-d-h, "to confess/acknowledge/give thanks"; the Hitpa'el of y-d-h: "to confess one's own iniquity/to make acknowledgment of," the reflexive indicating the personal ownership of the confession; avonam — "their iniquity/their guilt/their distortion"; the word avon from '-w-n, "to be bent/distorted/twisted" — iniquity as a moral crookedness, a distortion of what is straight] and the iniquity of their fathers [ve-et-avon avotam — וְאֵת-עֲוֺן אֲבֹתָם; the iniquity that has been inherited and extended must also be confessed — not merely personal sin but the multigenerational pattern], in their unfaithfulness with which they acted unfaithfully against me [be-ma'alam asher ma'alu-vi — בְּמַעֲלָם אֲשֶׁר מָעֲלוּ-בִי; ma'al — "unfaithfulness/treachery/breach of trust/sacrilege," from m-'-l, "to act treacherously/to trespass/to commit sacrilege"; the word specifically denotes breach of covenant or sacred trust — it is the technical term for betrayal of the divine-human relationship; the cognate construction ma'alam...ma'alu intensifies: the unfaithfulness of their unfaithfulness; asher ma'alu-vi — "with which they acted unfaithfully against me/in which they betrayed me"], and also that they walked contrary to me [ve-af asher halekhu imi be-qeri — וְאַף אֲשֶׁר-הָלְכוּ עִמִּי בְּקֶרִי; the sixth occurrence of qeri — the very attitude that generated the entire curse cycle must be confessed explicitly]:

**26:41** — I also [af-ani — אַף-אֲנִי; the same emphatic "I also" used in v.24 of YHWH matching their qeri; now it appears in a conditional clause: "I also had to..."] walked contrary to them [halakhti imahem be-qeri — הָלַכְתִּי עִמָּהֶם בְּקֶרִי] and I brought them into the land of their enemies [va-avi otam be-erets oyveikhem — וָאָבִא אֹתָם בְּאֶרֶץ אֹיְבֵיהֶם; the Hifil of b-w-' — "I caused them to enter/I brought them"; YHWH himself actively brought them into exile — it was not mere historical accident but divine action] — if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled [im-az yikana' levavam he-arel — אִם-אָז יִכָּנַע לְבָבָם הֶעָרֵל; yikana' — Niphal imperfect of k-n-', "to be humbled/be subdued/be brought low"; levavam — "their heart"; he-arel — "the uncircumcised one/the uncircumcised," from '-r-l, "to be uncircumcised"; levavam he-arel — "their uncircumcised heart" — the heart that has not received the spiritual circumcision corresponding to physical circumcision; the prophets use this image extensively: Jer 4:4, "circumcise your hearts"; Jer 9:25; Ezek 44:7,9; Deut 10:16; 30:6; the uncircumcised heart is the heart that is hard, insensitive, closed to YHWH's instruction — the opposite of the heart that hears and obeys; in exile, if that heart is finally humbled and opened], and if they accept the punishment of their iniquity [ve-azkiru et-avonam yirtsu — ?] wait — [ve-hem yirtsu et-avonam — וְהֵם יִרְצוּ אֶת-עֲוֺנָם; yirtsu — Qal imperfect of r-ts-h, "to be pleased/to satisfy/to pay off/to accept/to bear the consequences of"; hem — "they"; avonam — "their iniquity"; this phrase is difficult: yirtsu et-avonam — "they shall satisfy/bear/accept/pay off their iniquity"; the same root r-ts-h used of the land "being satisfied" of its sabbaths in vv.34–35; now the exiles "accept/bear the full weight of" their iniquity — they have stopped denying or minimizing it; they accept the justice of their punishment]:

**26:42** then I will remember my covenant with Jacob [ve-zakharti et-beriti Ya'aqov — וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי יַעֲקוֹב; zakharti — Qal perfect of z-k-r, "to remember/call to mind/be mindful of"; the divine remembering in Hebrew is not merely cognitive recall but active, purposive attention that leads to action; beriti Ya'aqov — "my covenant with Jacob"; the reverse-chronological order of the patriarchs — Jacob first, then Isaac, then Abraham — is unique here and has generated much discussion; the most likely explanation is that the reverse order (Jacob → Isaac → Abraham) moves from the most recent to the most ancient, intensifying the reach of divine memory back to the deepest roots of the covenant; or it may reflect a liturgical or theological order where Jacob-Israel is named first because this is specifically the covenant with the twelve-tribe nation], and also my covenant with Isaac [ve-af et-beriti Yitschaq — וְאַף אֶת-בְּרִיתִי יִצְחָק], and also my covenant with Abraham I will remember [ve-af et-beriti Avraham ezkor — וְאַף אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אַבְרָהָם אֶזְכֹּר; the three patriarchal covenants named individually — each is a distinct covenant, each encompasses the promise of land, seed, and blessing; YHWH will remember all three; ezkor — the Qal imperfect of z-k-r completes the sentence], and the land I will remember [ve-ha-aretz ezkor — וְהָאָרֶץ אֶזְכֹּר; the land is named as the fourth object of divine remembering — YHWH remembers not only the persons of the covenant but the land itself; the land has been lying desolate paying its sabbaths; YHWH will remember it too].

**26:43** But the land shall be abandoned by them [ve-ha-aretz te'azev mehem — וְהָאָרֶץ תֵּעָזֵב מֵהֶם; te'azev — Niphal imperfect of '-z-b, "to be abandoned/be forsaken/be left"; mehem — "by them/from them"; the land abandoned by its people during the exile period — this abandonment is not permanent but part of the redemptive process: the land must be emptied before it can be restored] and shall be paid its sabbaths in its being desolate from them [ve-hirtsah et-shabbetotehah be-hashammah mehem — וְהִרְצָת אֶת-שַׁבְּתֹתֶיהָ בְּהַשַּׁמָּה מֵהֶם; the sabbath-satisfaction theme from vv.34–35 revisited in this hopeful context: the desolation period is not wasted — the land is being restored, paid what it was owed], and they [the people] shall accept the punishment of their iniquity [ve-hem yirtsu et-avonam — וְהֵם יִרְצוּ אֶת-עֲוֺנָם; echoing v.41's conditional — what was conditional there ("if they accept") is now described as actually occurring; the repentant community truly accepts the justice of their punishment] because [ya'an u-ve-ya'an — יַעַן וּבְיַעַן; the doubled ya'an — "because and because/inasmuch as" — emphatic causal particle] they rejected my judgments [be-mishpatai ma'asu — בְּמִשְׁפָּטַי מָאָסוּ; ma'asu — Qal perfect of m-'-s, "to reject/despise"; the same verb as v.15 — the circle of causation is complete: their earlier rejection of judgments (v.15) is what caused all the curses; now acknowledged] and their soul abhorred my statutes [ve-et-chuqqotai ga'alah nafsham — וְאֶת-חֻקֹּתַי גָּעֲלָה נַפְשָׁם; ga'alah — Qal perfect of g-'-l, "to abhor"; nafsham — "their soul"; the final acknowledgment of the attitude described in v.15 — the soul that abhorred the statutes has brought about all of this; the confession must include naming the root attitude, not merely the outward actions].

**26:44** Yet even in this [ve-af gam-zot — וְאַף-גַּם-זֹאת; af gam — "and also/yet even/nevertheless" — the triple particle of concession and reversal; this phrase introduces the most remarkable turn in the chapter; despite everything — the utter desolation, the exile, the famine-cannibalism, the rotting in iniquity — "yet even this..."], when they are in the land of their enemies [be-heyotam be-erets oyveikhem — בִּהְיוֹתָם בְּאֶרֶץ אֹיְבֵיהֶם; be-heyotam — "in their being/while they are"], I will not reject them [lo-me'astim — לֹא-מְאַסְתִּים; me'astim — Qal perfect of m-'-s with third-person plural suffix: "I rejected them"; lo-me'astim — "I have not/will not rejected/reject them"; the same root m-'-s that Israel used to reject YHWH's statutes (v.43) is here negated of YHWH's relationship to Israel — YHWH will not reject them even though they rejected him; the moral asymmetry is the grace of the covenant] and I will not abhor them [ve-lo-ge'altim — וְלֹא-גְעַלְתִּים; ge'altim — Qal perfect of g-'-l with suffix: "I abhorred them"; the same root g-'-l that appeared in v.30 of the nafsho abhorring Israel and in v.43 of Israel's nafesh abhorring the statutes — here negated; lo-me'astim ve-lo-ge'altim — "I will not reject them and I will not abhor them" — these two verbs together capture the complete negative: no rejection, no revulsion; this is the covenant's indestructible floor] to destroy them completely [le-khalotam — לְכַלֹּתָם; kale — Pi'el infinitive of k-l-h, "to complete/finish/consume/destroy utterly"; le-khalotam — "to destroy them utterly/to bring them to an end completely"], to break my covenant with them [le-hafer beriti ittam — לְהָפֵר בְּרִיתִי אִתָּם; le-hafer — Hifil infinitive of p-r-r, "to break/annul/shatter"; the covenant that Israel broke (v.15) YHWH will never break; his covenant-faithfulness is not contingent on Israel's obedience in the ultimate sense — while disobedience triggers curses, it cannot sever the covenant relationship at its deepest root], for I am the LORD their God [ki ani YHWH Elohehem — כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם; the self-identification formula in third-person possessive — "their God" — affirming the relationship even while they are in exile, even while they suffer the covenant curses; the formula is the covenant's unbreakable identity statement].

**26:45** And I will remember for them the covenant of the ancestors [ve-zakharti lahem berit rishonim — וְזָכַרְתִּי לָהֶם בְּרִית רִאשֹׁנִים; zakharti lahem — "I will remember for them/to their benefit"; lahem — "for them," the preposition of benefit; berit rishonim — "the covenant of the former ones/the covenant of the forefathers/the covenant of the ancestors"; rishonim — "former/first/ancient ones," the patriarchs as the originating generation; the covenant of the ancestors is the Abrahamic covenant, the promise of land, seed, and blessing that predates and undergirds the Sinaitic covenant], whom I brought out from the land of Egypt before the eyes of the nations [asher hotseti otam me-erets Mitsraim le-einei ha-goyyim — אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִי אֹתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לְעֵינֵי הַגּוֹיִם; le-einei ha-goyyim — "before the eyes of the nations/in the sight of the peoples"; the Exodus was a public, international event — performed before the watching nations; YHWH's honor and reputation among the nations is bound up with his treatment of Israel; to destroy Israel completely would be to un-do the Exodus before the watching world, making the redemption appear temporary and the Redeemer unreliable], to be their God [li-heyot lahem le-Elohim — לִהְיוֹת לָהֶם לֵאלֹהִים; the purpose clause — the Exodus happened "in order to be their God"; the covenant formula is the telos of the Exodus event itself]: I am the LORD [ani YHWH — אֲנִי יְהוָה; the final, unadorned self-declaration; stripped to its essence: I am YHWH; the name alone carries the full weight of all that has been said — the creator, the covenant-maker, the judge, the redeemer, the one who will not forget].

---

**26:46** These are the statutes [elleh ha-chuqqim — אֵלֶּה הַחֻקִּים; the formal colophon begins; elleh — "these are," the demonstrative that marks a conclusion; ha-chuqqim — "the statutes," the inscribed decrees] and the judgments [ve-ha-mishpatim — וְהַמִּשְׁפָּטִים; the pair chuqqim-mishpatim — the two categories of divine law — statutes and judgments, as throughout the chapter] and the teachings [ve-ha-Torah — וְהַתּוֹרָה; ha-Torah — "the Torah/the Teaching/the Instruction/the Law," from y-r-h, "to direct/throw/instruct/point the way"; the single word Torah encompasses all of divine instruction — it is the comprehensive term; the colophon identifies the entire Levitical legislation as Torah] that the LORD gave [asher natan YHWH — אֲשֶׁר נָתַן יְהוָה; natan — Qal perfect of n-t-n, "to give/grant"; the legislation is a gift from YHWH, not a human composition; the passive divine origin] between himself and the sons of Yisra'el [beino u-vein benei Yisra'el — בֵּינוֹ וּבֵין בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; beino — "between him/between himself"; the covenant is bilateral — between two parties, YHWH and Israel; though asymmetrical in power, it involves genuine relationship and mutual commitment; u-vein benei Yisra'el — "and between the sons of Yisra'el"] on Mount Sinai [be-har Sinai — בְּהַר סִינַי; the locative conclusion: everything in the Holiness Code traces its origin to Sinai; cf. 25:1 where the Jubilee legislation was similarly grounded at Sinai; the entire edifice of Levitical law is Sinaitic in origin] by the hand of Moshe [be-yad Moshe — בְּיַד מֹשֶׁה; be-yad — "by the hand of/through the agency of"; the mediator is named; YHWH gave the Torah, Moshe was the channel through which it passed to Israel; this is the standard formula for prophetic transmission (cf. Exod 9:35; Num 4:37, 45; 9:23); be-yad Moshe functions as both a historical authentication and a theological statement about the nature of prophecy and covenant mediation].

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Overview
Leviticus 26 has a formally structured architecture:
- **Prologue: Covenant conditions** (vv.1–2): anti-idolatry command + sabbath/sanctuary observance
- **Blessing pericope** (vv.3–13): four domains — agricultural (vv.4–5), security (vv.6–8), demographic/covenantal (vv.9–10), divine Presence (vv.11–13)
- **Curse pericope, five stages** (vv.14–39):
  - Stage 1: Disease + agricultural failure (vv.14–17)
  - Stage 2: Drought + pride broken (vv.18–20)
  - Stage 3: Wild animals (vv.21–22)
  - Stage 4: Sword + plague + siege famine (vv.23–26)
  - Stage 5: Utter desolation, cannibalism, exile, exile-terror (vv.27–39)
- **Restoration pericope** (vv.40–45): confession → humbling → divine remembering → covenant reaffirmation
- **Colophon** (v.46)

### Key Vocabulary
- **qeri** (vv.21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41): "contrary/hostile indifference/treating YHWH's acts as random"; the chapter's diagnostic keyword for the attitude that drives the escalating curse cycle. Seven occurrences.
- **sheva al-chattoteikhem** (vv.18, 21, 24, 28): "sevenfold for your sins"; the sevenfold multiplier of covenantal discipline; the number seven as covenant-intensifier.
- **nafshi** (vv.11, 30): YHWH's "soul/innermost being"; used first in the promise that YHWH's nafshi will not abhor them (v.11), then in the curse that his nafshi abhors them (v.30) — the conditional horror that the blessing promised to avoid.
- **g-'-l** (vv.11, 30, 43, 44): "to abhor/loathe"; the verb of visceral rejection; used of YHWH toward Israel (vv.11, 30) and of Israel toward YHWH's statutes (v.43); negated in v.44 as the final covenant guarantee.
- **m-'-s** (vv.15, 43, 44): "to reject/despise"; parallel to g-'-l; Israel rejected YHWH's judgments; YHWH will not reject Israel.
- **ve-hithalakhti be-tokhekhem** (v.12): "I will walk about in your midst"; the Eden-resonance of the Hitpa'el of halakh (cf. Gen 3:8).
- **qomemiyut** (v.13): "uprightness/with head erect"; hapax legomenon — occurs only here; the freed person walking tall.
- **im-be-chuqqotai telekhu** (v.3): "if in my statutes you walk"; the chapter's conditional opening for blessing.
- **ve-zakharti et-beriti Ya'aqov** (v.42): "I will remember my covenant with Jacob"; the reverse-chronological order Jacob → Isaac → Abraham is unique in the Hebrew Bible.
- **lo-me'astim ve-lo-ge'altim** (v.44): "I will not reject them and I will not abhor them"; the covenant's indestructible floor; among the most consoling phrases in Torah.
- **berit rishonim** (v.45): "covenant of the ancestors/the former ones"; the Abrahamic covenant as the bedrock beneath the Sinaitic.
- **ve-ha-Torah** (v.46): the colophon names the entire body of Levitical legislation as "Torah" — the comprehensive term.

### The Reverse-Patriarchal Order in v.42
The listing Jacob → Isaac → Abraham (rather than the expected Abraham → Isaac → Jacob) is unique in the Hebrew Bible. Three main interpretations: (1) The order moves from most recent to most ancient, intensifying the reach of divine memory back through time; (2) Jacob = the nation Israel, so the covenant with the nation is named first, then its individual ancestral foundations; (3) A liturgical or poetic inversion for rhetorical effect, emphasizing that YHWH's memory reaches all the way back to the founding covenant.

### The Covenant Curses and Ancient Near Eastern Treaty Structure
The structure of Lev 26 closely parallels the Hittite suzerain-vassal treaty pattern (identified by K. A. Kitchen, G. Mendenhall, M. G. Kline): (a) historical prologue (implicit in vv.1–2, explicit in vv.13, 45); (b) stipulations (the entire body of Levitical law); (c) blessings for compliance (vv.3–13); (d) curses for non-compliance (vv.14–39); (e) provision for restoration after judgment (vv.40–45). The Deuteronomy 28–30 parallel expands this same structure at much greater length.

### Theological Note: The Land's Sabbaths and Exile
Verses 34–35 and 43 establish one of the most remarkable theological claims in the Hebrew Bible: the Babylonian exile is the land's sabbath-rest. The land that Israel refused to give its sabbath (Lev 25) receives it forcibly through exile. This is not mere poetry — 2 Chr 36:21 cites it as the explicit theological explanation of the seventy-year Babylonian exile, making Jeremiah's seventy years (Jer 25:11–12; 29:10) the precise repayment of seventy missed sabbatical years from the settlement period. The mathematics of covenant consequence is here given historical actualization.

### The Uncircumcised Heart (v.41)
Levav he-arel — "the uncircumcised heart" — is a key term in biblical theology connecting physical circumcision (the covenant sign) with an inner transformation (Deut 10:16; 30:6; Jer 4:4; Jer 9:25; Ezek 44:7). The image is of a heart that has not received the spiritual counterpart of the physical rite — closed, hard, insensitive to YHWH's direction. The promise of Deut 30:6 ("the LORD your God will circumcise your heart") and Ezek 36:26 ("I will give you a new heart") are the prophetic extensions of what is here described negatively as the problem of the uncircumcised heart.
